title: Blending coloured pulps
subtitle: Mixing pre-dyed pulp in the vat for tinted and two-tone handmade sheets
excerpt: The simplest way to make coloured handmade paper is to blend a coloured pulp into the vat. The colour distributes evenly through the sheet rather than sitting on the surface. Using two distinct coloured pulps added in sequence creates two-tone effects: poured separately they pool; stirred together they blend.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923, public domain), chapter on coloured papers. Internet Archive scan available. The specific blend-in-vat approach is synthesised from contemporary craft documentation consistent with Hunter's colour principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A colour mixed into the vat furnish rather than applied to the finished sheet, producing a colour that is integral to the paper fibre.
A handmade sheet made by pouring two differently coloured pulps into the vat before pulling, creating a blended or side-by-side colour effect.

content.text: Coloured handmade paper most often starts with coloured tissue paper (cheap, easily available, pre-dyed) or coloured cotton linter pulp. Coloured tissue is torn into small pieces, soaked, and blended into a slurry: the dye in the tissue transfers into the water and coats the cotton fibre, producing a
pulp tint
. The colour is not permanent in an archival sense, but it is even and integral to the sheet.
Keep the blender unplugged when adding fibre or rinsing. Drain the vat before reaching in.
What you need
Coloured tissue: two or three sheets of coloured tissue at 17 to 20 gsm in the chosen colour. Tear into 50 mm pieces, soak in hot water for 5 minutes.
Base cotton pulp: prepared cotton linter slurry at normal furnish consistency in the vat.
Making a single-colour tinted sheet
Blend the soaked coloured tissue in the blender with 1 litre of warm water until smooth. The slurry will be strongly coloured.
Add the coloured slurry to the vat of cotton pulp. Stir thoroughly.
Pull a test sheet. The colour will be lighter in the dry sheet than in the wet slurry because the water carries some colour away during draining.
Adjust by adding more coloured tissue slurry if the tint is too pale.
Making a two-tone sheet
Prepare two separate pulp slurries in different colours. Ladle them into the vat at the same time, one on each side.
For a
two-tone sheet
with a visible colour boundary, pull the sheet immediately without stirring.
For a blended gradient, give one gentle circular stir before pulling. The colours will intermingle toward the centre without fully mixing.
Variations
Use concentrated watercolour or calligraphy ink instead of coloured tissue to tint the vat. The effect is similar but the dye does not contribute additional fibre.
